learning.whatWillYouLearn: Prerequisites: Arrays, Binary Search, Sorted Arrays, Divide and Conquer, Time Complexity
learning.whyDoesThisExist: Imagine two hospitals each maintaining sorted patient age records. Instead of merging both lists, efficiently find the middle age among all patients using binary search.
learning.realWorldAnalogy: Imagine two hospitals each maintaining sorted patient age records. Instead of merging both lists, efficiently find the middle age among all patients using binary search.
learning.whenToUseThis: Use Binary Search on Partition whenever you need the median of two sorted arrays in logarithmic time.
learning.signals: Whenever two sorted arrays require finding the median in logarithmic time, think of Binary Search on Partition.
learning.expectedLearningOutcome: I will know when to use this pattern.

problem.problemStatement: Given two sorted arrays nums1 and nums2 of sizes m and n respectively, return the median of the two sorted arrays. The overall run time complexity must be O(log(min(m, n))).

thinkingProcess.naiveIdea: Merge both sorted arrays into one sorted array, then directly compute the median.
thinkingProcess.keyInsight: Rather than merging both arrays, partition them so that the left half contains exactly half of the total elements. If every element on the left is less than or equal to every element on the right, the correct partition has been found.
thinkingProcess.ahaMoment: The correct partition makes every left element less than or equal to every right element.
thinkingProcess.patternRecognition: Whenever two sorted arrays require finding the median in logarithmic time, think of Binary Search on Partition.
thinkingProcess.whyItWorks: Binary search is performed only on the smaller array. Every partition check takes constant time, giving O(log(min(m,n))) complexity.
thinkingProcess.howToRecognizeSimilar: Whenever two sorted arrays require finding the median in logarithmic time, think of Binary Search on Partition.
commonWrongPaths: Confusing even and odd total lengths.
Merging arrays despite the logarithmic requirement.

hints: Always binary search the smaller array.
Partition both arrays into left and right halves.
Compare boundary elements of the partitions.
commonMisconceptions: Confusing even and odd total lengths.
Merging arrays despite the logarithmic requirement.

pattern.name: Binary Search on Partition
pattern.recognitionSignals: Whenever two sorted arrays require finding the median in logarithmic time, think of Binary Search on Partition.

approaches.title: Merge Both Arrays
approaches.idea: Merge both sorted arrays into one sorted array, then directly compute the median.
approaches.dryRun: Merge then find the middle element.

approaches.title: Two Pointer Merge Without Extra Array
approaches.idea: Traverse both arrays using two pointers until the median position is reached without storing the merged array.
approaches.dryRun: Stop once the median index is reached.

approaches.title: Binary Search on Partition
approaches.idea: Always perform binary search on the smaller array. Partition both arrays such that the total elements on the left equal half of the combined length. Adjust the partition until every left-side element is less than or equal to every right-side element.
approaches.intuition: Binary search is performed only on the smaller array. Every partition check takes constant time, giving O(log(min(m,n))) complexity.
algorithm: Ensure nums1 is the smaller array.
Binary search the partition in nums1.
Compute the corresponding partition in nums2.
If partitions are valid, compute the median.
Otherwise move the binary search left or right.
approaches.dryRun:
nums1=[1,3]
nums2=[2]
Partition1=1
Partition2=1
Left={1,2}
Right={3}
Median=2

code.javascript: JavaScript implementation follows the same binary-search partition logic as the Java solution by always searching on the smaller array and comparing partition boundaries.

visualization.description: Partition both arrays into equal left and right halves.
steps: Find matching partition in the second array.

revisionNotes: Always binary search the smaller array.

interviewFollowUps: Why binary search only the smaller array?
How is the partition formula derived?
What happens if one array is empty?
Can this be extended to k sorted arrays?
interviewTips: State O(log(min(m,n))) time and O(1) space.

cheatSheet.keyObservation: The correct partition makes every left element less than or equal to every right element.
cheatSheet.algorithm: Binary Search → Partition → Validate → Compute Median.

importantPoints: Works for odd and even lengths.
commonMistakes: Confusing even and odd total lengths.
Merging arrays despite the logarithmic requirement.
